name: System Design Scenarios
description: Frontend architecture decisions — pick the best approach.

questions.scenario: You're building a shopping cart for an e-commerce site. Users can add items, change quantities, and checkout. The cart must persist if the user refreshes the page.
questions.question: Where should the cart state live?
options: Only in React component state (useState)
Server state with React Query, no local state
Client state synced to localStorage, pushed to server on checkout
Only in a cookie
questions.explanation: A shopping cart needs to persist across refreshes (localStorage) but doesn't need constant server sync until checkout. Keep it in client state (Context/Zustand + localStorage) and sync to the server when the user checks out.

questions.scenario: Your app has a table showing 50,000 order records. Users need to search, sort, and browse all records. The API supports pagination.
questions.question: What pagination approach would you choose?
options: Load all 50,000 records at once for instant filtering
Server-side cursor-based pagination with virtual scrolling
Client-side pagination after loading all data
Infinite scroll loading 10 records at a time
questions.explanation: With 50K records, loading all data is impractical. Cursor-based server pagination with virtual scrolling renders only visible rows, keeping memory low and performance high. The server handles sorting/filtering.

questions.scenario: Your app shows a user profile on many screens. The profile data rarely changes (maybe once a week). Each screen currently makes its own API call to fetch it.
questions.question: What caching strategy should you use?
options: No caching — always fetch fresh data
Cache with a long staleTime (e.g., 30 min), revalidate on focus
Store in localStorage permanently, never refetch
Use a service worker to intercept all requests
questions.explanation: Since profile data rarely changes, use a data-fetching library (React Query/SWR) with a long staleTime. The data is served from cache instantly, and revalidated in the background on window focus to stay fresh.

questions.scenario: Users can 'like' a post in your social feed. The API takes 200-500ms to respond. You want the UI to feel instant.
questions.question: How should you implement the like button?
options: Disable the button until the API responds
Use optimistic updates — update UI immediately, roll back on error
Show a loading spinner on the button
Queue likes and send them in batch every 5 seconds
questions.explanation: Optimistic updates make the UI feel instant: update the like count and heart icon immediately, send the API request in the background, and only roll back if it fails. This is standard for social interactions.

questions.scenario: You're building a real-time chat feature. Messages should appear instantly when sent by other users. The app has ~1,000 concurrent users.
questions.question: What communication approach should you use?
options: Poll the server every 500ms for new messages
WebSocket connection for real-time bidirectional communication
Long polling with 30-second timeouts
Users manually refresh to see new messages
questions.explanation: WebSockets provide real-time bidirectional communication, ideal for chat. Unlike polling, there's no wasted requests when idle, and messages arrive instantly. At 1K users, a single WebSocket server handles the load easily.

questions.scenario: Your React app has 20+ pages. Each page needs different data from the API. Some data is shared across pages. You need to decide where data fetching lives.
questions.question: Where should data fetching logic go?
options: In each component's useEffect directly
Custom hooks per data domain using a fetching library (React Query/SWR)
A single global store that fetches everything on app load
In getServerSideProps for every page
questions.explanation: Custom hooks per domain (useUser, useOrders) using React Query/SWR give you automatic caching, deduplication, and revalidation. Shared data is fetched once and cached. Each page only fetches what it needs.

questions.scenario: You need a search autocomplete that queries an API as the user types. Results should appear fast, but you don't want to overload the server with requests on every keystroke.
questions.question: What's the best implementation approach?
options: Fire an API call on every keystroke
Debounce input (300ms), cancel previous requests, cache recent results
Only search when the user presses Enter
Preload all possible results on page load
questions.explanation: Debouncing waits until the user pauses typing, reducing API calls. Cancel in-flight requests (AbortController) to avoid race conditions. Cache recent queries so revisiting a term is instant.

questions.scenario: Your team wants to gradually roll out a new checkout flow. Some users should see the old flow, some the new one. You need to be able to toggle it without a deploy.
questions.question: How should you implement this?
options: Create a separate branch and deploy it to a different URL
Use a feature flag service to control which users see the new flow
Use A/B testing with Math.random() in the component
Deploy both versions and let users choose in settings
questions.explanation: A feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or a simple config API) lets you target users by percentage, segment, or ID. You can toggle the flag instantly without deploying, and roll back if issues arise.

questions.scenario: Your React app has many nested routes. Some routes have complex data loading. You want to catch rendering errors gracefully without crashing the whole app.
questions.question: Where should you place error boundaries?
options: One error boundary at the root of the app only
At route boundaries and around critical UI sections like data widgets
Around every single component
Error boundaries aren't needed — use try/catch everywhere
questions.explanation: Place error boundaries at route boundaries so a crash in one page doesn't break others. Add them around critical widgets (charts, data tables) that might fail independently. Too many boundaries adds complexity without benefit.

questions.scenario: Your SPA needs authentication. After login, the user gets a JWT access token (expires in 15 min) and a refresh token (expires in 7 days). You need to store them securely.
questions.question: Where should you store the tokens?
options: Both tokens in localStorage
Access token in memory, refresh token in an httpOnly cookie
Both tokens in a regular cookie
Access token in sessionStorage, refresh token in localStorage
questions.explanation: Storing the access token in memory (JS variable) protects it from XSS. The refresh token in an httpOnly cookie is inaccessible to JavaScript. On page refresh, use the refresh token cookie to get a new access token silently.
